original_abstract: Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) have catalyzed a revolution in computer vision, enabling unprecedented advancements across diverse tasks such as image classification, object detection, and video prediction. By leveraging autonomous feature learning and hierarchical expression, CNNs have largely superseded traditional machine learning approaches that rely on manual feature engineering. This review provides a comprehensive exploration of the CNN landscape, beginning with a fundamental analysis of core components, including convolutional and pooling layers, activation functions, batch normalization, dropout, and fully connected architectures. We examine the evolution of seminal models from early frameworks like AlexNet and VGG to sophisticated modern architectures such as ResNet, GoogLeNet, and the attention-based SENet, while also considering the efficiency of lightweight models like MobileNet for resource-constrained environments. Furthermore, we categorize and evaluate object detection methodologies, distinguishing between two-stage and one-stage detectors, and discuss the emerging frontiers of spatio-temporal modeling for video prediction. By synthesizing current research trends, this paper identifies persistent challenges—such as the demand for extensive labeled data, computational complexity, and the need for improved model interpretability—and outlines future research directions to guide the development of next-generation visual intelligence systems.
humanized_abstract: Over the past decade, Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) have become a backbone of most computer vision tasks. Starting from image classification, object detection, up to video prediction, a plethora of advancements in the field of computer vision have been introduced via the CNNs. Due to their capability to learn important features from the data, CNNs often use hierarchical representations to accomplish a wide variety of computer vision tasks in an autonomous way, leaving traditional approaches using hand crafted features behind. This review gives an overview of various aspects of the CNNs. In this context, it begins with an elementary discussion on the various constituent parts of the network, namely convolutional and pooling layers, activation functions, batch normalization, dropout, and fully connected layers. Following which it moves to a thorough discussion of various state-of-the-art CNN models from basic architectures such as AlexNet and VGG to advanced architectures including ResNet, GoogLeNet, SENet and small parametric networks like MobileNet for limited resource-scenario. After that, an in-depth analysis for the object detection approaches have been provided distinguishing between the two stage and one stage detectors, along with exploring some of the future works on the spatio-temporal models in video prediction task. In conclusion, after summarizing various works in the context of visual intelligence based on CNNs in this review, by putting together various findings from the existing works, several common open challenges such as requiring large amounts of labelled data, the complexity of the networks and the black-box nature of the networks, have been introduced and it is followed by the future directions.
